page.title: DESIGNING WITH AI
page.subtitle: A systems-level workflow for solo designers — rebuilt around Claude Code at Wren AI.
intro.product.body: In early 2026, a designer at Anthropic declared that the traditional design process was dead. At the same time, I was a solo product designer at Wren AI — one person holding product design for a B2B AI platform, with 16 hours of my week swallowed by meetings and 3–4 projects running in parallel at any moment. This case study documents the 11-step AI workflow I built to make that actually possible.
intro.role.body: Senior Product Designer at Wren AI. Also, by necessity: PM, researcher, prototyper, motion designer, illustrator, technical writer, dogfooding lead. The workflow is how one person covers ~6–8 FTE of traditional scope without losing craft.
s01.title: Achievement
s01.body: These are the raw numbers behind my week. Not aspirational — actual throughput since the pipeline stabilized. The workflow is what closes the gap between what's asked and what's humanly possible in 40 hours.
s01.m1.label: Equivalent headcount
s01.m1.note: Of traditional design-adjacent roles now absorbed by the pipeline — PM, researcher, prototyper, UI, motion, illustrator, tech writer, analyst.
s01.m2.label: Concurrent projects
s01.m2.note: Held in active progress at any given week — not queued, not on pause. Each in a different stage of the pipeline.
s01.m3.label: Meeting load
s01.m3.note: Standups, reviews, stakeholder syncs, user calls — roughly 40% of the work week is structurally non-negotiable.
s01.m4.label: Deep work remaining
s01.m4.note: All design, research, prototyping, writing, and video production happens inside this window — roughly 6 hours per project per week.
s01.quote: "The design process isn't dead — it's inverted. The designer stops being the maker and starts being the orchestrator of makers."
s01.quote.attr: — Reflection after one quarter of running this pipeline
s02.title: Why this workflow exists
s02.moment.body: Early 2026. An Anthropic designer publishes a post arguing that the traditional design process — discovery → research → wireframe → mockup → spec → handoff — is obsolete. Models can generate working interfaces faster than a designer can sketch one. The artifact (the Figma file) is no longer where the value lives.
s02.moment.body2: I read it at a B2B AI startup where I was the only product designer. I didn't have the luxury of disagreeing. The reality was already pressing against me:
s02.li1: A single designer covering product design, visual design, prototyping, research, and brand for an entire product.
s02.li2: 16 hours/week structurally committed to meetings that couldn't be eliminated.
s02.li3: 3–4 workstreams running concurrently — Agent mode, Embedded Threads, Thread Tracing, and Design System maintenance.
s02.li4: Engineers who were already using Claude Code daily — if my handoffs weren't compatible with their workflow, I was the bottleneck.
s02.li5: A startup budget that ruled out hiring — and ruled out most paid tools above the free tier.
s02.reframe.body: The old question — "How do I get more time to design?" — had no answer. The week was full. The new question became: "How do I make every hour of mine count for five?" That only worked if I stopped being the person making artifacts and started being the person directing a system that made them.
s02.reframe.body2: Claude Code became that system. Not as a tool I reach for occasionally, but as the central orchestration layer — the thing specs, prototypes, research summaries, marketing videos, Notion updates, and product docs all flow through. Below is the pipeline I ended up with.
s03.title: The 11-step AI pipeline
s03.body: Each step is a Claude Code skill, a plugin, or a scripted handoff between tools. None of them are "AI helps me do X." They are: AI does X, I review and redirect. The vertical spine below reads chronologically — from analytics at the top, through spec, journey, prototype, Figma, handoff, video, brand, dogfooding, PM ops, and external docs.
s03.step0.title: Behavior analysis & backtest
s03.step0.story: A Claude Code skill ingests analytics events, session recordings, and support tickets. It produces a behavior report with funnel drops, friction patterns, and anomalies — then backtests them against the last round of design changes to show what actually moved.
s03.step0.output: Weekly behavior report + design delta review
s03.step1.title: Spec writing & feature brainstorm
s03.step1.story: A one-line brief from the CEO becomes a full spec — problem statement, scope, edge cases, acceptance criteria, risk list — inside a Claude Code skill that knows the product surface and the existing design system. I edit the shape of the argument, not the prose.
s03.step1.output: PRD-grade spec with edge cases & criteria
s03.step2.title: User journey as HTML
s03.step2.story: The spec feeds into a skill that produces an interactive HTML user journey — stages, actions, emotions, pain points, and opportunity moments. It's shareable as a link, survives review edits, and becomes the single source of truth that every downstream step references.
s03.step3.title: Prototype from journey + Storybook
s03.step3.story: I feed the journey HTML + the team's Storybook component docs into a skill. It generates a working HTML/CSS/JS prototype — routing, states, interactions, fake data. I deliberately don't read what framework it used. As long as it runs in the browser and behaves like the journey describes, it's doing its job.
s03.step4.title: Iterate in Claude Code
s03.step4.story: I fix the prototype directly in Claude Code — rework flows, tighten states, fill empty/error/loading branches. This is where interaction design happens now: in running code, not in Figma frames. The prototype becomes the design artifact of record.
s03.step5.title: Sync to Figma → Figma Make → back to prototype
s03.step5.story: I pull the prototype into Figma to re-tidy layout and correct component usage against the design system. Then I drop it into Figma Make to explore display states and test alternative layouts at speed. Whatever wins comes back into Claude Code as a prompt to update the prototype. This is the only step where I still touch pixels directly — and it's the shortest.
s03.step6.title: Handoff: Figma + prototype → engineer → Claude
s03.step6.story: Engineers receive Figma and a working prototype. They feed the prototype into their own Claude Code session to generate production code — treating my prototype as executable spec, not as a mockup to reinterpret. Handoff ambiguity drops to near zero because there's nothing to translate.
s03.step7.title: Marketing video via Remotion
s03.step7.story: Claude Code wraps the same prototype with Remotion to produce a launch video — scripted scene timing, on-screen callouts, voiceover stubs. Marketing gets a ready-to-edit MP4 without me opening After Effects. The design artifact and the marketing artifact share the same source.
s03.step8.title: Brand: mascot, banners, teaser video
s03.step8.story: Gemini, Weave, and Lovart (free tiers only) cover the brand layer — company mascot illustrations, campaign banners, short teaser videos. I treat this as a daily 15-minute ritual, not a project. The cost is zero; the output covers what we used to pay an illustrator for.
s03.step9.title: Dogfooding → summary → task assignment
s03.step9.story: The same user journey that drove the prototype also generates a dogfooding checklist. Internal testers fill it in; a Claude Code skill consolidates the findings, ranks them by severity, proposes fixes, and drafts task assignments by owner. By Monday morning, I have a sprint-ready backlog I didn't have to hand-write.
s03.step10.title: PM ops: Notion sync & workload math
s03.step10.story: Claude updates the Notion product roadmap against the backlog, recalculates each person's workload, and surfaces overbooked weeks before they blow up. The weekly roadmap review becomes a 10-minute review of Claude's draft, not a 2-hour spreadsheet session.
s03.step11.title: External product documentation
s03.step11.story: The journey map + a Claude Code skill + a documentation plugin produce the customer-facing docs — help center pages, API reference stubs, release-note drafts. The same source that drove the prototype now drives the words users read when the feature ships.
s04.title: The headcount math
s04.h3: How many people this would have been
s04.body: Conservative FTE estimates, assuming a mid-sized product org where each role is specialized. "FTE" here means the fraction of one full-time person's week the step would normally require for a single active project. Multiply by 3–4 for parallel projects, then divide by the reality that I actually deliver it alone — that's the compression factor the pipeline has to achieve.
s04.row0.out: Weekly behavior report, funnel deltas, design backtest
s04.row1.out: PRD-grade spec, edge cases, acceptance criteria
s04.row3.out: Runnable HTML/CSS/JS prototype from journey + Storybook
s04.row4.out: Iterated prototype — flows, states, error branches
s04.row5.out: Design-system-aligned layout via Figma + Figma Make
s04.row6.out: Prototype-as-spec handoff; engineer's own Claude codes it
s04.row7.out: Remotion-rendered launch video from the prototype
s04.row8.out: Mascot, banners, teaser videos (free-tier AI only)
s04.row9.out: Dogfooding checklist, ranked findings, task drafts
s04.row10.out: Notion roadmap sync, workload math, overbooking flags
s04.row11.out: Help center, API reference, release notes
s04.total.label: Equivalent headcount per active project
s04.multi.body: A single project carries ~6.4 FTE of traditional scope. Three-to-four running in parallel would nominally require ~20 FTE, but shared overhead (one PM serving all projects, one design system, one motion system) collapses that to roughly 6–8 FTE of real hiring you'd need to match the output. That's the department the pipeline is standing in for.
s05.title: Running 3–4 projects in parallel
s05.body: 16 hours of meetings is the constraint. The remaining 24 hours is what the pipeline exists to protect. Here's where every hour actually goes.
s05.bar.title: 40-hour week breakdown
s05.legend.meetings: Non-negotiable: standups, reviews, stakeholder syncs, user calls
s05.legend.deep: Claude-orchestrated: spec, prototype, video, docs, PM ops
s05.copy.body: Without the pipeline, 24 hours is 3 days of solo-designer throughput — enough for a slice of one project, not four. With the pipeline, those same 24 hours are 24 hours of orchestration. Each hour I spend reviewing Claude's output shifts an entire sub-project forward.
s05.h3.alloc: How the 24 hours splits across projects
s05.alloc.body: A representative week. Not every project is in the same stage — which is what makes parallel work survivable. Agent mode is in handoff; Embedded Threads is mid-prototype; Thread Tracing is in dogfooding; the design system is on maintenance mode.
s05.proj1.name: Agent Mode
s05.proj2.name: Embedded Threads
s05.proj3.name: Thread Tracing
s05.proj4.name: Design System
s05.insight: Six hours per project per week. The only way that produces shippable work is if each of those hours is aimed at judgment — which direction, which trade-off, which edit — rather than at production. The pipeline absorbs production; I absorb direction.
s06.title: The stack
s06.h3: Every tool, and what it's for
s06.body: The stack is deliberately small and deliberately cheap. Claude Code is the orchestration layer; everything else is a specialist that Claude drives. Tools tagged in yellow are free-tier only — swapped out before they ever become a recurring invoice.
s06.t1.desc: The central layer. Every step feeds through it — specs, journeys, prototypes, dogfooding, Notion, docs. Custom skills for each workflow.
s06.t2.desc: Custom, repeatable workflows — one per step. Each skill encodes the prompt, the output format, and the tool chain so the pipeline runs the same way every time.
s06.t3.desc: Thin adapters to Notion, Storybook, analytics, and the docs site. Plugins are how steps 09–11 reach outside the local environment.
s06.t4.desc: Still the source of truth for visual alignment and design-system components. Used narrowly — tidying and correcting, not originating.
s06.t5.desc: State variants and layout bake-offs at speed. Winning direction goes back into Claude Code as a prompt to update the prototype.
s06.t6.desc: Not a deliverable — an input. Claude reads Storybook to generate prototypes that already use the right components.
s06.t7.desc: Marketing videos rendered from the prototype. Claude writes scenes, timing, and callouts; Remotion renders the MP4.
s06.t8.desc: Mascot, banners, teaser videos. Rotated across free tiers to stay within quota. Zero recurring cost.
s06.t9.desc: Product roadmap, backlog, workload tracking. Claude writes; I review. The weekly roadmap review shrinks from 2 hours to 10 minutes.
s07.title: Principles & reflections
s07.principles.body: Six rules the pipeline enforces. They aren't preferences — they're the conditions under which one person can carry this scope without the work degrading.
s07.p1.title: Claude is the orchestrator, not a tool
s07.p1.body: Claude Code doesn't assist the workflow — it runs the workflow. Every other tool is something Claude operates. I operate Claude.
s07.p2.title: Prototype-first, Figma-second
s07.p2.body: The prototype is the design artifact of record. Figma is where I tidy and align, not where I originate. The artifact engineers build from is runnable code.
s07.p3.title: Free tools wherever possible
s07.p3.body: Paid-tool creep kills startup workflows. Brand visuals use only free tiers of Gemini / Weave / Lovart. If a tool can't stay free, it gets replaced.
s07.p4.title: Don't read what you don't have to
s07.p4.body: When Claude generates prototype code, I don't audit the framework choice. If it runs and matches the journey, it ships. Deciding less is how I hold more.
s07.p5.title: Every artifact shares a source
s07.p5.body: The journey map drives the prototype, the dogfooding checklist, and the docs. Reuse of source is what makes 11 steps feel like one.
s07.p6.title: Meetings are the real bottleneck
s07.p6.body: Design time stopped being scarce. Attention did. The pipeline is worth it only because it buys back the 24 hours that meetings don't already own.
s07.h3.reflect: Where AI carries me vs. where it can't
s07.reflect.body: A year in, the line has become clear. AI absorbs production — making, drafting, rendering, summarizing. It does not absorb judgment, taste, or the parts of the job that depend on being a specific person in a specific room.
s07.can.title: Production, at any volume
s07.can.body: Specs, journeys, prototypes, iterations, videos, docs, dogfooding summaries, Notion updates, workload math. Work that is high-volume, pattern-shaped, and reviewable — the pipeline does all of it, and would do ten times more if the week allowed.
s07.cant.title: Judgment, trust, presence
s07.cant.body: Stakeholder empathy in a tense review. The strategic framing of which problem is worth designing for. The user call where the real insight comes from a tone of voice, not a transcript. The political read of which proposal can land this quarter. AI doesn't sit in those rooms. I do.
s07.closing: "If the traditional design process is dead, then what replaces it isn't less design — it's a designer who can direct an entire studio with one week of their own time."
